title: Bond Markets Are Volatile. Here's How to Choose Between Government and Corporate Debt.
author: Sara Appino
description: The article compares two bond ETFs for intermediate-term investors: IEI (Treasury bonds) offers safety with lower volatility but higher fees, while IGIB (corporate bonds) provides higher yields and lower costs with greater diversification. The choice depends on economic outlook—IEI acts as insurance during market stress, while IGIB is better for income-focused investors in stable conditions.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Positioned as a defensive, lower-volatility option with no credit risk. Higher expense ratio (0.15%) and lower yields (3.60%) are drawbacks, but it provides genuine portfolio insurance during economic uncertainty with lower maximum drawdown (13.90% vs 20.60%).

Offers superior practical value with lower expense ratio (0.04%), higher dividend yield (4.80%), and extensive diversification across 2,963 holdings. Better for income-focused investors, though vulnerable if economic conditions deteriorate and corporate credit spreads widen.

title: VGIT vs IEI: The maturity gap that changes your rate exposure
author: Seena Hassouna
description: VGIT and IEI are both intermediate-term U.S. Treasury ETFs with key differences: VGIT offers a lower expense ratio (0.03% vs 0.15%), higher yield (3.8% vs 3.6%), and broader maturity range (3-10 years), making it more rate-sensitive. IEI has a narrower maturity band (3-7 years), lower volatility, and slightly better 5-year returns. The choice depends on portfolio positioning and desired rate exposure.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: VGIT is presented favorably with a significantly lower expense ratio (0.03%), higher dividend yield (3.8%), larger AUM ($48.5B), and broader maturity exposure (3-10 years). These features provide better value and more duration exposure for rate-sensitive strategies.

IEI is positioned as a solid alternative with advantages including lower maximum drawdown (-14.6% vs -16.05%), slightly better 5-year growth ($1,023 vs $1,014), and a tighter, more predictable maturity band (3-7 years) that reduces rate sensitivity uncertainty.

title: Better Bond ETF: Fidelity's FIGB vs. iShares' IEI
author: Robert Izquierdo
description: Fidelity's FIGB and iShares' IEI offer different approaches to bond investing. FIGB provides broader diversification across investment-grade bonds with higher yields (4.1%) but charges a higher expense ratio (0.36%) and carries more volatility. IEI focuses exclusively on intermediate Treasury bonds with lower costs (0.15%), greater safety, and larger liquidity ($18.7B AUM), making it ideal for conservative investors prioritizing capital preservation.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: FIGB offers higher dividend yield (4.1%), better 1-year returns (5.9%), broader diversification across 180 holdings in multiple bond sectors, and active management aimed at outperforming the broader market. These features appeal to income-focused investors willing to accept higher fees and volatility.

IEI is positioned favorably for conservative investors due to its lower expense ratio (0.15%), lower volatility (beta 0.69), maximum capital preservation through Treasury-only focus, and significantly larger AUM ($18.7B) providing superior liquidity. Its passive management keeps costs minimal.

title: Vanguard BND Offers Broader Bond Mix Than BlackRock's IEI
author: Adé Hennis
description: The Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND) and iShares 3-7 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEI) both offer core bond exposure but differ in approach. BND provides broader investment-grade bond diversification with a lower 0.03% expense ratio, while IEI focuses exclusively on intermediate-term U.S. Treasuries with a 0.15% expense ratio. Both funds show similar one-year returns (~4.2%), but BND has experienced greater long-term drawdown over five years.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: BND is highlighted for its significantly lower expense ratio (0.03% vs 0.15%), broader diversification across 15,000 securities, and strong AAA-rated bond holdings (72%). The article positions it as a better choice for long-term, cost-conscious investors seeking balanced bond exposure.

IEI is presented as a viable alternative for investors preferring concentrated U.S. Treasury exposure with minimal credit risk and AA-rated bonds. However, it carries a higher expense ratio and shows worse five-year performance, making it a more specialized choice rather than an outright positive recommendation.

title: FIGB Offers Higher Yield Than IEI With Broader Bond Mix but Lower 1-Year Return
author: Sarah Sidlow
description: The Fidelity Investment Grade Bond ETF (FIGB) offers a higher dividend yield and broader bond portfolio with 689 holdings across government and corporate debt, but charges double the expense ratio of the iShares 3-7 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEI) and has underperformed IEI over the past four years with larger drawdowns. IEI provides a safer, more focused approach with exclusive U.S. Treasury exposure and lower costs.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: FIGB offers advantages (higher yield of 4.15%, broader diversification with 689 holdings, 50% government backing) but is offset by disadvantages (higher expense ratio of 0.36%, lower 1-year return of 2.2%, larger maximum drawdown of -15.62%, underperformance vs IEI over 4 years). The mixed performance profile warrants a neutral stance.

IEI demonstrates superior characteristics including lower expense ratio (0.15%), better 1-year return (2.7%), smaller maximum drawdown (-10.86%), exclusive U.S. Treasury backing offering maximum safety, and stronger 4-year performance ($941 vs $881 for FIGB). The article identifies it as 'the safer of the two' with a favorable risk-return profile.
